description: Detailed commercial account connected to the flax trade network of Yosef ibn ʿAwkal. Dated: Shaʿbān 429H (May–June 1038). Ibn ʿAwkal has distributed bales of flax (and some pepper and sukk, a medicine) among several agents, among them Qāsim b. Zāʿī, ʿUmar b. Ghawkal/Jawkal, Ibn al-Jazūla, Ibn al-ār, ʿAbdūn al-Nashāʾ, and Abū Isḥaq. He records how much each owes in salfa (shipping fees) and clarifies which storage costs they are and are not responsible for. The total cargo handled amounts to at least 133 bales. According to Gil, Ibn ʿAwkal directed this account to an associate in Alexandria. Each entry begins with the verb אחלתה (aḥaltuhu), meaning "I transferred" or "I assigned" via ḥawāla, a formal legal instrument of debt transfer in Islamic commercial law. When a creditor or debtor uses a ḥawāla, he is transferring his claim or obligation to a third party, essentially saying, instead of paying me directly/instead of my paying you directly, the debt should be collected from this third person. By saying אחלתה עלי פלאן, he means, "I transferred/assigned the collection to so-and-so. This financial instrument allowed merchants to move value across distances without physically transporting money. For some of the technical terms, I have relied in my translation on Gil, "The Flax Trade in the Mediterranean in the Eleventh Century A.D. as Seen in Merchants’Letters from the Cairo Geniza," Journal of Near Eastern Studies, 63 (2004): 81–96. MR
tags: flax, Yosef b. Awkal, trade, hawala
description_he: חשבון מסחרי מפורט ברשת סחר הפשתן של יוסף אבן עוקאל. שעבאן 429 להג'רה (מאי-יוני 1038). מפרט חלוקת מאות חבילות פשתן בין סוכנים שונים, עמלות שינוע ועלויות אחסנה, באמצעות מנגנון ההוואלה.
